analogy: Think of "A Shirt in the Market" through the chapter's own opening images: 86 Social and Political Life A Shirt in the Market This chapter tells us the story of a shirt ! It begins with the production of cotton and ends with the sale of the shirt. We shall see that a chain of markets links the... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
A Shirt in the Market is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 86 Social and Political Life A Shirt in the Market This chapter tells us the story of a shirt !

The text explains that it begins with the production of cotton and ends with the sale of the shirt.

The text explains that we shall see that a chain of markets links the producer of cotton to the buyer of the shirt in the supermarket.

The text explains that buying and selling takes place at every step in the chain.

The text explains that 87 Did Swapna get a fair price on the cotton?

The text explains that where do you think large farmers would sell their cotton?

The text explains that how is their situation different from Swapna?

The text explains that a cotton farmer in Kurnool Swapna, a small farmer in Kurnool (Andhra Pradesh) grows cotton on her small piece of land.

The text explains that the bolls of the cotton plant are ripe and some have already burst, so Swapna is busy picking cotton.

The text explains that the bolls, which carry the cotton in them, do not burst open all at once so it takes several days to harvest the cotton.

The text explains that once the cotton is collected, instead of selling it at Kurnool cotton market, Swapna and her husband take the harvest to the local trader.

The text explains that at the beginning of the cropping season, Swapna had borrowed ` 2,500 from the trader at a very high interest rate to buy seeds, fertilisers, pesticides for cultivation.

The text explains that at that time, the local trader made Swapna agree to another condition.

The text explains that he made her promise to sell all her cotton to him.

The text explains that cultivation of cotton requires high levels of inputs such as fertilisers and pesticides and the farmers have to incur heavy expenses on account of these.

The text explains that most often, the small farmers need to borrow money to meet these expenses.

The text explains that faced with such pressures from the buyers, the garment exporting factories, in turn, try to cut costs.

The text explains that the spellings of names in this map have been taken from various sources.

The text explains that 1999. Growth and Recession in Small-Scale Industry: A Study of Tamil Nadu Powerlooms Economic and Political Weekly.

The text explains that world Health Organization, Essential medicines and health products, http://www.who.int/medicines/services/inn/en/ www.cehat.org/rthc/policybrieffinal.pdf www.in.undp.org (Sustainable Development Goals  SDGs) www.infochangeindia.org Zubaan.

The text explains that poster Women: A Visual History of the Womens Movement in India.

The text explains that the responsibility for the correctness of internal details rests with the publisher.

The text explains that the territorial waters of India extend into the sea to a distance of twelve nautical miles measured from the appropriate base line.

The text explains that the administrative headquarters of Chandigarh, Haryana and Punjab are at Chandigarh.

The text explains that the interstate boundaries amongst Arunachal Pradesh, Assam and Meghalaya shown on this map are as interpreted from the North Eastern Areas (Reorganisation) Act, 1971,but have yet to be verified.

The text explains that the external boundaries and coastlines of India agree with the Record / Master Copy certified by Survey of India.

The text explains that 6 The state boundaries between Uttarakhand & Uttar Pradesh, Bihar & Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh & Madhya Pradesh have not been verified by the Goverments concerned.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 8: A Shirt in the Market

schemes.name: At
schemes.point: At times, the government helps the cooperatives by buying cloth from them at a reasonable price. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch8 pg 4)

schemes.name: For
schemes.point: For instance, the Tamil Nadu government runs a Free School Uniform programme in the state.The government procures the cloth for this programme from the powerloom weavers... (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch8 pg 4)

schemes.name: Similarly
schemes.point: Similarly, the government buys cloth from the handloom weavers cooperatives and sells it through stores known as Co-optex. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch8 pg 4)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 86 Social and Political Life and 8 CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to A Shirt in the Market; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain A Shirt in the Market through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 86 Social and Political Life and 8 CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
